answer:
We test for invalidity by trying to make all premises true and the conclusion false.  
If the conclusion \(L \supset K\) is false, then \(L\) is true and \(K\) is false.  
With \(K\) false, \(N \lor K\) requires \(N\) true; then \(N \supset \sim M\) forces \(M\) false.  
Now \((K \cdot L) \supset M\) becomes \((F \cdot T) \supset F\), which is \(F \supset F = T\), so all premises are true with \(L\) true, \(K\) false, \(N\) true, \(M\) false.  

This matches option D.  

\boxed{D}
